name: perseus-vault-bitemporal-gauntlet
description: A rigorous bi-temporal reasoning gauntlet for Perseus Vault. Unlike the 4-scenario transaction-time mini-benchmark, the gauntlet exercises all THREE temporal axes against SQL:2011 semantics: (1) transaction time via as_of ('what did we believe at T'), (2) valid/application time via valid_at ('what was actually true in the world at T, as we understand it now'), and (3) full bi-temporal via bitemporal ('as of what we believed at tx_at, what was true at valid_at'). Scenarios cover the hard cases that flat and single-axis memory stores (Mem0, Cognee) and valid-time-only stores (Zep/Graphiti) get wrong: retroactive correction, proactive/future-dated facts, out-of-order arrival, and audit-grade point-in-time reconstruction. All timestamps are expressed as day-offsets from an anchor 'now' the harness resolves at runtime, so absolute times vary run-to-run but PASS/FAIL verdicts are stable.

scenarios.narrative: We recorded on day 0 that Alice is a 'staff engineer'. On day 0 we ALSO learn (retroactively) that she was actually a 'senior engineer' from day -300 until she was promoted on day -60. Both facts are recorded now but describe different world-periods.

checks.why: In the world at day -150 she was a senior engineer
By day -10 she had been promoted
Before day -300 no title fact was valid

scenarios.narrative: On day 0 we record that Bob earns 120k now, and that a raise to 150k becomes effective on day +30 (a future-dated / proactive fact). A valid-time query for today must return 120k; a query for day +45 must return 150k even though that world-instant has not happened yet.

checks.why: Today the raise has not taken effect
The future-dated raise is valid from day +30

scenarios.narrative: On day 0 (t0) we believed a project was 'on-track'. On day 0 + a few seconds (t1) we learn it was actually 'at-risk' the whole time and correct the record with a retroactive valid_from in the past. Transaction time and valid time now DIVERGE: as_of(t0) still returns the old belief ('on-track'); valid_at(past) returns the corrected truth ('at-risk').

checks.why: At t0 we still BELIEVED on-track
As we understand it NOW, it was at-risk the whole time
As of what we believed at t0, the day -10 status was on-track
As of current knowledge, the day -10 status was at-risk

scenarios.narrative: Facts arrive out of chronological order. We first record the NEWER fact (opus-4-8, valid from day -5), then later record an OLDER fact we forgot to log (opus-4-7, valid day -90 to day -5). Valid-time queries must still stitch the timeline correctly regardless of recording order.

checks.why: At day -40 the model was opus-4-7 even though logged second
At day -1 the model was opus-4-8

scenarios.narrative: A fact with a CLOSED valid period that is not superseded by a successor. User biked from day -200 to day -30, then the fact ends (they stopped commuting entirely; no replacement fact). valid_at inside the window returns 'bike'; valid_at after the window returns found=false (the fact stopped being true and nothing replaced it).

checks.why: After the window closed with no successor
